graded_decisions.news_since: WWDC 2026 (Jun 9): Siri AI beta-only, relies on Google Gemini, limited monetization. KeyBanc: lacking in context of high bar for advancements (Yahoo Finance/GuruFocus, Jun 9). Stock fell ~8% post-WWDC.
graded_decisions.comment: AI thesis invalidated by WWDC delivery gap. Stock had already rallied 25% from March lows heading into event — no margin for error. New CEO Ternus unproven. Position should have been trimmed or hedged pre-event.

graded_decisions.news_since: Oracle cloud miss (Jun 11) sparked sector AI-capex selloff; Xbox restructuring/layoffs (Jun 11, Motley Fool); FTC broadened investigation into AI/cloud/bundling; $190B FY2026 capex compressed margins to 67.6%, lowest since 2022.
graded_decisions.comment: Entry at $425 appears to have been near the tail of a momentum spike ($412→$460, May 27–Jun 1). Multiple compounding headwinds arrived simultaneously. AI revenue ($37B/yr) intact but near-term margin compression and FTC scrutiny dominate sentiment. No stop or invalidation level triggered exit.

graded_decisions.news_since: SharonAI 6-year compute deal (Jun 12); KKR/NVDA $10B Helix Digital Infrastructure deal; Vera CPU marketed to Chinese clients for August delivery. Oracle cloud miss (Jun 11) spread AI-capex doubt to semis broadly.
graded_decisions.comment: AI infrastructure demand thesis intact — new deals confirm datacenter dominance. Price stabilized this week (+0.19%) after sharp Jun 5 selloff. Entry slightly above current price; thesis holds but patience required.

graded_decisions.news_since: Broad market recovered from Jun 5 flash selloff; S&P 500 held 725 support and bounced.
graded_decisions.comment: SPY position acting as intended ballast. Only green position in the book. Broad market strength partially offsets tech-specific headwinds.

patterns: AI-event anchor risk: both AAPL and MSFT held as AI plays with no pre-event hedges or trim plans; both punished severely post-announcement (WWDC disappointment, Oracle-triggered AI-capex doubt).

Intra-basket correlation too high: AAPL (-6.73%), MSFT (-8.67%), NVDA (-5.30%) all declined simultaneously on the same AI-sentiment rotation — the portfolio moves as a single AI-factor bet, not a diversified basket.

Momentum-chasing at top: MSFT entry near $425 occurred near the tail of a spike from $412 to $460 (May 27–Jun 1); entry appears to have been into momentum rather than confirmed breakout or mean reversion.
No exit discipline visible: all three underwater positions held without evidence of invalidation stops or thesis re-evaluation triggers; drawdowns allowed to compound to -5% to -9%.
First review: no decision journal exists yet — daily routine must begin logging decisions.jsonl entries so future reviews can grade specific session choices.
suggested_adjustments: Cap AI/tech sector weight at 55% of portfolio market value; trigger a rebalance review when it drifts above 60% (currently ~74% of long positions).
Skip new longs in any name with a major product event (earnings, developer conference, analyst day) within 5 trading days; enter only after post-event price settles for 2+ sessions.
Require 2+ independent outlets confirming thesis before initiating or adding to a position; a single bullish article is not a sufficient entry signal.
Set a per-position invalidation stop at -8% from average entry price; exit fully when a contrary news catalyst (e.g., WWDC AI disappointment) directly contradicts the original thesis.
summary: Week -1.93% vs SPY +0.69%, VT +1.77%, SSO +1.11% — portfolio underperformed all three passive benchmarks by 2.6 to 3.7 percentage points. AAPL and MSFT drove the loss: WWDC AI disappointment and Oracle-triggered AI-capex scrutiny hit both names hard. NVDA stabilized. SPY was the sole winner (+1.27%). Core problem: ~75% of capital in correlated AI/big-tech names with no event hedges, no invalidation stops, and no source diversity in thesis construction. Four rule adjustments proposed addressing concentration, event timing, source requirements, and stop discipline.
